content:
6 min read
2 hours ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

I have shipped seven Next.js apps in the last three years. I like Next.js. I don’t have a vendetta. I’m telling you that because what I’m about to say has been twisting on me for six months and I want it to land as the considered take it is, not as a “Next.js is dead” hot post.
I rebuilt one of those seven apps in TanStack Start last month. The dev experience was so noticeably better that I’m now reconsidering my default for new projects. Not abandoning Next. Reconsidering.
This is the post I’d want from someone in my position. Not the “five reasons” listicle. The “what actually changed when I used it” version. Includes when I’d still pick Next and when I think TanStack Start is genuinely the better answer in 2026.
what TanStack Start actually is
It’s a full-stack React framework built on TanStack Router and Vite. It hit stable in early 2026 and the npm downloads are now around 6 million per week, which is the part where “experiment” stops being a fair characterization.
The pitch in two beats:

• Type-safe routing with file-based conventions, but the types are real (you get autocompletion on params, search params, loader returns).

• Vite, not Turbopack, not Webpack. Local dev is fast. HMR is fast. Cold start is fast.
There’s no RSC layer. There’s no “server vs client component” mental model. SSR is regular SSR with full hydration, with optional streaming where you want it. The framework is, deliberately, less magic.
If you have spent any time confused by which file boundaries Next infers as server vs client and where the RSC fence draws itself, that paragraph might already have your attention.
the moment that got me
The rebuild was a content-heavy dashboard. The Next.js version had grown into a tangle of 'use client' boundaries and unstable_cache calls and "wait, why is this re-fetching." I knew the codebase well. I still got lost in it twice a week.
I ported it to TanStack Start over a long weekend. Two things stood out by Sunday night:
Routing types were finally what I wanted. Defining a route looks like:
export const Route = createFileRoute('/users/$userId')({
loader: async ({ params }) => {
return await db.users.findFirst({ where: { id: params.userId } });
},
component: UserPage,
});The params.userId is typed. The loader's return value is typed. When you use Route.useLoaderData() inside UserPage, the data type flows through. When you call router.navigate({ to: '/users/$userId', params: { userId: '5' } }) from somewhere else, you get autocompletion and errors at compile time if you pass the wrong shape.
I’ve wanted this in Next.js for four years. Typed routes finally landed but are still less complete than what TanStack ships out of the box.
Local dev was noticeably faster. Cold start on my machine: ten seconds with Next, two with Start. HMR: 800ms with Next, 300ms with Start. These are small numbers but they pile up over a workday. The “ergonomic” win is felt across the day.
That’s the moment. Not a benchmark. A four-day rebuild where I kept going “huh, that was easier than I expected.”
where TanStack Start genuinely wins
Stuff I’d point at without qualifying:
Routing type safety. No contest. Best in the React ecosystem right now.
Mental model overhead. The lack of an RSC layer is a feature if you’ve been bitten by RSC edge cases. You think in components and SSR. That’s it.
Vite-first builds. Faster dev loop, smaller production bundles for some workloads. The plugin ecosystem you already know works. No “is this a Vite plugin or a Webpack plugin” tax.
Loaders. Route loaders run before component render. They’re typed. They cache. They invalidate. They’re the route-data story TanStack Query users already know, but for the page itself, not just data fetching inside components.
Content sites. Benchmarks I’ve seen show 20–30% faster load times for content-heavy pages versus Next 16 with default config. I haven’t independently verified this on a representative app, but the pattern is consistent across the writeups I trust.
where Next.js still wins
I want to be precise about this because the conversation gets one-sided fast on Twitter:
Server Components. If you want the RSC model (server components that fetch directly, no client JS for static parts, the streaming + Suspense + Server Actions trio), Next is currently the most mature implementation. TanStack Start does not do this. It’s a different architecture.
Image / Font / Script optimization. Next has spent five years polishing <Image>, next/font, and built-in head management. TanStack Start has equivalents but they're younger. For a content site that lives or dies by Lighthouse scores, Next still has the edge.
The Vercel deployment experience. I have philosophical feelings about lock-in. I also acknowledge that deploying a Next app to Vercel and having edge caching, image CDN, ISR, and analytics just work is, empirically, the smoothest deploy story in the JS world. TanStack Start deploys fine to Vercel, Netlify, Cloudflare, your own server, but the level of out-of-the-box integration is lower.
Ecosystem and hiring. Next has dramatically more libraries, examples, Stack Overflow answers, and engineers who know it. That’s not a small advantage when you need to staff a team or pick a tutorial.
Server Actions and forms. If you’ve bought into the Server Actions + useActionState pattern (and I have, see my previous posts), that's a Next feature. TanStack Start has server functions but the ergonomics are slightly different.
my actual recommendation in mid-2026
For new projects, the decision tree:
Pick TanStack Start if:

• The app is interactive (dashboards, internal tools, SaaS UIs).

• Type-safe routing matters to your team.

• You want to think in plain React + SSR, not RSC.

• You’re deploying somewhere non-Vercel.

• Cold-start latency in dev matters to you.
Pick Next.js if:

• You’re building a content site where SEO and Lighthouse scores rule.

• You want the RSC model and have a team that already understands it.

• You’re deploying to Vercel and want every optimization wired up.

• You need every library and integration that exists in the React ecosystem.
For an existing Next app: stay on Next. Migration cost is real and the upside is mostly DX, not user-visible perf. Don’t rewrite for vibes.
For a new app where you’d otherwise default to Next out of habit: at least open a TanStack Start scaffold and try it for an hour. If it doesn’t grab you, you’ve lost an hour and you go back to Next. If it does, you’ve found your next default.
what the framework conversation is really about
This is the broader thing I want to say.
For five years the React framework conversation was “Next.js or you’re behind.” That made sense when the alternatives were genuinely weaker. It stopped making sense as soon as TanStack Start hit stable. The conversation is now “Next.js or TanStack Start,” and the choice depends on what you’re building.
That’s healthy. The React ecosystem has been too monocultured for too long. Having a second serious framework with a different opinion (lean, type-safe, no RSC magic) makes everyone’s reasoning sharper. It also gives you a real alternative when the Next team makes a roadmap decision you don’t love.
I don’t think Next is going anywhere. I do think the next generation of senior React devs will have used both, and that’s a good thing.
anyway
If you’re a Next developer who’s never opened a TanStack Start project, the homework for this week is one hour with npx create-tsrouter-app or the official starter. See how routing feels. See how the loader-into-component flow lands for you. Decide if it's a fit for your next project.
You don’t have to switch. You just have to know what you’re picking when you reach for Next out of muscle memory.
Sources:

• TanStack Start vs Next.js in 2026: Should You Actually Switch?

• Next.js vs TanStack Start: Framework Comparison for 2026

• Next.js 16 vs TanStack Start (2026): Performance, Memory Leaks & Migration Guide

• TanStack Start vs Next.js vs Remix 2026
